coredata.dc:title: Electrochemical recognition of tryptophan enantiomers using self-assembled diphenylalanine structures induced by graphene quantum dots, chitosan and CTAB
coredata.dc:creator: Guo, Lili
Bao, Liping
Yang, Baozhu
Tao, Yongxin
Mao, Huihui
Kong, Yong
coredata.dc:description: Molecular self-assembly offers a promising route to the preparation of advanced materials for the construction of novel chiral sensing devices, and the inspiration for the development of such systems is often derived from simple biological models. Diphenylalanine (FF), an extensively studied short peptide, can self-assemble into highly ordered nano-/micro-structures. Here we report the electrochemical recognition of tryptophan enantiomers using three FF self-assembled structures produced in the presence of graphene quantum dots (GQDs), chitosan (CS) and cetyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB). Although the difference in the peak potentials of the enantiomers is very small, enantiomeric differences can be detected by the magnitude of the DPV current signals. The recognition efficiencies of the three self-assembled materials are different, due to the different structures formed during the self-assembly process.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Electrochemical recognition
Tryptophan enantiomers
Self-assembled diphenylalanine
Simple building blocks

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of GCEs modified with self-assembled FF structures
Preparation of a GCE modified with electrodeposited FF/CS films

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: FESEM images of (A) lyophilized FF, (B) FF/GCE, (C) FF-GQDs/GCE, (D) FF-CS/GCE and (E) FF-CTAB/GCE.

FT-IR spectra of (A) FF-GQDs, (B) FF-CS and (C) FF-CTAB. (D) Schematic showing the formation of CS-induced FF self-assembly. (E) CVs of GCEs modified with GQDs-, CS- and CTAB-induced FF self-assemblies and a bare GCE in 0.1M KCl containing 5mM [Fe(CN)6]4−/3−.

DPVs of 0.5mM l-Trp and d-Trp included with (A) FF/GCE, (B) FF-GQDs/GCE, (C) FF-CS/GCE and (D) FF-CTAB/GCE. (E) Optimized structures of FF/l-Trp and FF/d-Trp complexes by DFT. (F) DPVs of 0.5mM l-Trp and d-Trp included with a GCE modified with electrodeposited FF/CS films. (G) FESEM image of GCE modified with electrodeposited FF/CS films. (H) CVs of FF-CS/GCE in 0.1M KCl containing 5mM [Fe(CN)6]4−/3− from the 1st to the 20th cycle.

head.ce:title: Electrochemical recognition of tryptophan enantiomers using self-assembled diphenylalanine structures induced by graphene quantum dots, chitosan and CTAB

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Advanced Catalysis and Green Manufacturing Collaborative Innovation Center, Changzhou University, Changzhou 213164, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Advanced Catalysis and Green Manufacturing Collaborative Innovation Center

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Molecular self-assembly offers a promising route to the preparation of advanced materials for the construction of novel chiral sensing devices, and the inspiration for the development of such systems is often derived from simple biological models. Diphenylalanine (FF), an extensively studied short peptide, can self-assemble into highly ordered nano-/micro-structures. Here we report the electrochemical recognition of tryptophan enantiomers using three FF self-assembled structures produced in the presence of graphene quantum dots (GQDs), chitosan (CS) and cetyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB). Although the difference in the peak potentials of the enantiomers is very small, enantiomeric differences can be detected by the magnitude of the DPV current signals. The recognition efficiencies of the three self-assembled materials are different, due to the different structures formed during the self-assembly process.

ce:section.ce:para: Chiral recognition of amino acids is of great significance in the life sciences [1]. Among the proposed techniques for chiral recognition of amino acids, electrochemistry has become increasingly attractive since it can easily convert chiral molecular recognition events into discernible changes in electrochemical responses [2], and applications of voltammetry with modified electrodes for chiral recognition of tryptophan have been reported by various groups [3–6]. However, as far as we are aware, little progress has been made in electrochemical chiral recognition using simple biological building blocks such as amino acids, peptides and nucleic acids.

Among the molecular building blocks, peptides are particularly attractive due to their low cost, simplicity, and thermal and structural stability [7]. More importantly, peptides can be easily decorated with chemical and biological elements and may self-assemble into various functional nanostructures and ordered molecular chains [8]. Diphenylalanine (FF) is a short dipeptide that can be organized into a variety of ordered structures including nano/microtubes [9], nanowires [10] and honeycomb architectures [11] via either spontaneous self-assembly or induced self-assembly. Although self-assembled FF nanostructures have been used in electrochemical sensors [12], little attention has been paid to the use of FF-based self-assembled materials for electrochemical chiral recognition. In fact, peptides have been used successfully as a chiral stationary phase to resolve various chiral compounds [13].

Here we report the electrochemical recognition of tryptophan (Trp) enantiomers by FF-based self-assembled structures induced by graphene quantum dots (GQDs), chitosan (CS) and CTAB. The nano-/micro-structures of the resulting self-assembled FF-GQDs, FF-CS and FF-CTAB are shown to differ. Owing to their different structures, they also exhibit different chiral recognition efficiencies.

SEM images were recorded on a Supra55 field-emission scanning electron microscope. FT-IR spectra were measured by a FTIR-8400S spectrometer. Electrochemical measurements were carried out in a conventional three-electrode system consisting of a GCE (3mm in diameter) modified with FF or self-assembled FF structures as the working electrode, a platinum foil (10×5mm) as the counter electrode and a saturated calomel electrode as the reference electrode.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of GCEs modified with self-assembled FF structures
ce:section.ce:para: GQDs were prepared by direct pyrolysis of citric acid [14], and then 20mg of the resulting GQDs were dissolved in 10mL water. Solutions of CS and CTAB at the same concentration were prepared by dissolving 100mg of CS and CTAB in 50mL 0.1M acetic acid and water.

FF can be easily dissolved in 1,1,1,3,3,3-hexafluoro-2-propanol (HFIP), and thus a FF stock solution was prepared by dissolving the lyophilized FF in HFIP at a concentration of 50mgmL−1. The stock solution was diluted with water to a final concentration of 3mgmL−1. Next, 20μL of the GQDs dispersion was added to the diluted FF solution, and then 5μL of the mixture was dropped onto the GCE surface and allowed to dry at 30°C for 6h to obtain the FF-GQDs/GCE. FF-CS/GCE and FF-CTAB/GCE were prepared using a similar procedure. For comparison, 5μL of diluted FF was dropped onto a GCE and allowed to dry at 30°C to produce a GCE modified with spontaneously self-assembling FF (FF/GCE).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of a GCE modified with electrodeposited FF/CS films
ce:section.ce:para: A GCE modified with electrodeposited FF/CS films was prepared via a two-step potentiostatic method. A negative potential of −0.5V was applied to the GCE for the deposition of CS from 2mgmL−1 CS dissolved in 0.1M acetic acid. After 150s, the CS/GCE was transferred into the 3mgmL−1 solution of FF, and a positive potential of +0.5V was applied to the CS/GCE for another 150s for the subsequent deposition of FF.

Electrochemical recognition of Trp enantiomers was studied using GCEs modified with the induced self-assembled FF structures or the spontaneously self-assembled FF structure. These electrodes were immersed in 25mL 0.1M PBS (pH7.0) containing 0.5mM l- or d-Trp at 15°C for 60s, respectively, and then the DPVs of the included l- and d-Trp were recorded. The ratio of peak currents (I L-Trp/I D-Trp) was calculated in order to assess the recognition efficiency.

Fig. 1 shows the FESEM images of lyophilized FF, FF/GCE, FF-GQDs/GCE, FF-CS/GCE and FF-CTAB/GCE. The lyophilized FF exhibits a nanorod structure, which is inclined to aggregate owing to the intrinsic H-bonds between the FF chains (Fig. 1A). After the lyophilized FF had been dissolved and dropped onto the GCE surface, FF microtubes with diameters ranging from 0.5 to 1.5μm can be observed (Fig. 1B), which might be due to the spontaneous self-assembly of FF. Previous studies have also revealed that FF can be self-assembled into ordered tubular structures by a combination of π-π stacking of aromatic residues and H-bonds [8,9]. Since the isoelectric point (PI) of FF is 5.68, the FF diluted with water is negatively charged. From the electrostatic point of view, the oxygen-containing groups on the GQDs are adverse to the combination of FF and GQDs, however, the intermolecular H-bonds between the amino NH2 hydrogen of FF and the carboxyl oxygen of GQDs could be responsible for the GQDs-induced FF self-assembly, resulting in the formation of a goose-foot-like structure (Fig. 1C). The PI of CS is 6.3, and thus CS is positively charged in 0.1M acetic acid (pH3.0). When FF is self-assembled using CS as the inducer, both electrostatic attractions and H-bonds play crucial roles in the induced self-assembly of FF, and the resulting stucture is composed of interwoven nanofibers of about 400nm (Fig. 1D). CTAB is a cationic surfactant which is also positively charged when dissolved in water. However, the morphology of FF-CTAB is quite different from that of FF-CS, and larger hollow tubes ranging from 1 to 1.5μm are characteristic of FF-CTAB (Fig. 1E). Since electrostatic attractions exist in both FF-CS and FF-CTAB, the different structures might be attributed to the lack of H-bonds between FF and CTAB. The structure of the induced self-assembled FF material is clearly dependent on the inducers used, and a larger surface area containing chiral sites is exposed for FF-CS compared with FF-GQDs and FF-CTAB.

Fig. 2A shows the FT-IR spectra of the FF-GQDs, FF and GQDs. For FF, two peaks at 1687 and 1610cm−1 are characteristic of the amide I region, and another two peaks at 3257 and 1550cm−1 are attributed to the anti-symmetric stretching and bending of NH, respectively. For the GQDs, two small peaks located at 1766 and 1716cm−1 are due to the anti-symmetric and symmetric stretching vibrations of CO. For the induced self-assembly of FF-GQDs, the characteristic peaks of FF at 3257 and 1550cm−1 drift to 3259 and 1556cm−1, while the characteristic peaks of GQDs at 1766 and 1716cm−1 are downshifted to 1764 and 1710cm−1, respectively. The drift of the characteristic peaks could be ascribed to the formation of H-bonds (NHOC) between FF and the GQDs [15]. The FT-IR spectra of FF-CS are shown in Fig. 2B. CS displays several peaks at approximately 1155cm−1, 1080 and 1027cm−1, which can be ascribed to the saccharine structure. These characteristic peaks of CS in FF-CS are red-shifted to 1150, 1073 and 1023cm−1, respectively. Meanwhile, the peaks of FF at 3257 and 1550cm−1 are blue-shifted to 3261 and 1558cm−1, respectively, on the spectrum of FF-CS, which is most likely due to the formation of H-bonds (NHOH) between the amino groups of FF and the hydroxyl groups of CS. Fig. 2C shows the FT-IR spectra of FF-CTAB, FF and CTAB. For CTAB, two peaks at 2918 and 2848cm−1 are assigned to the stretching vibrations of CH, corresponding to the characteristic absorption bands of the CTAB main chain. For FF-CATB, the sharp peaks in the region 1500–1400cm−1 can be ascribed to the deformation vibrations of CH2 and CH3 of the incorporated surfactant. It is of particular interest that the positions of the characteristic peaks of FF and CTAB scarcely change in the FF-CTAB spectrum, indicating that the induced self-assembly of FF by CTAB is accomplished via electrostatic attractions, not H-bonds.

The self-assembly mechanisms are studied taking FF-CS as an example. The positive charge on the CS is beneficial for the self-assembly of negatively charged FF; on the other hand, FF can be combined with CS through the formation of H-bonds between the amino groups of FF and the hydroxyl groups of CS. That is to say, the synergistic effects of electrostatic interactions and H-bonds play important roles in the formation of the induced FF-based self-assemblies except in the case of FF-CTAB. A schematic illustration of the formation of the CS-induced FF self-assembled structure is shown in Fig. 2D.

The CVs of GCEs modified with these induced FF self-assemblies and a bare GCE are recorded in 5mM [Fe(CN)6]4−/3− (Fig. 2E). A pair of well-defined redox peaks is observed for the bare GCE. The peak currents are decreased at the modified GCEs, which could be ascribed to the inferior electrical conductivity of the induced FF self-assembled structures. It is noteworthy that the FF-GQDs/GCE exhibits the lowest electrochemical activity toward the redox of [Fe(CN)6]4−/3−, since the lowest peak currents are observed on the FF-GQDs/GCE. This phenomenon can be explained by considering the fact that CS and CTAB are positively charged, while the GQDs are negatively charged under the given conditions. The electrostatic repulsion between the GQDs and the negatively charged [Fe(CN)6]4−/3− leads to significantly decreased peak currents.

Chiral recognition of Trp enantiomers is studied using GCEs modified with FF self-assembled structures. As shown in Fig. 3A, a discernible recognition is achieved for the FF/GCE, indicating that Trp enantiomers can be recognized by spontaneously self-assembled FF. However, the recognition ability of FF is not as high as expected, since the value of I L-Trp/I D-Trp is only 1.18. The low recognition efficiency using FF might be attributed to the aggregated FF chains and the consequently decreased number of active sites. Using GCEs modified with the induced FF self-assembled materials produces higher I L-Trp/I D-Trp values: 2.71 (FF-GQDs), 3.72 (FF-CS), and 2.74 (FF-CTAB) (Figs. 3B–D). The enhanced recognition efficiency with the induced FF self-assembled materials might be attributed to the increased number of chiral sites in the induced self-assemblies compared with the spontaneous self-assembly, since the intrinsic H-bonds among the FF chains are broken due to the incorporation of various inducers for FF self-assembly and consequently the surface containing chiral sites is easily exposed. Of the three induced self-assembled structures, FF-CS possesses the largest surface area containing active sites, as shown in Fig. 1C–E, resulting in the highest recognition efficiency.

It is interesting to find that for all the self-assembled materials studied, the peak current of l-Trp is much higher than that of d-Trp, demonstrating that more l-Trp is included in these induced FF self-assemblies than its enantiomer. In other words, the induced self-assemblies exhibit higher affinity toward l-Trp than toward d-Trp. To obtain theoretical information regarding the selective combination of the Trp enantiomers with the FF self-assemblies, the structures of the ground states of the FF/l-Trp and FF/d-Trp complexes were optimized by density functional theory (DFT). As shown in Fig. 3E, an H-bond can be formed between the H atom of NHCO on FF and the O atom of OH on l-Trp, and the distance between H and O is calculated to be 1.623Å. As reported by Schuster and Mikenda [16], HO distances are rejected if the respective OH distances are shorter than 0.90Å, while an H-bond is extremely weak or absent beyond 2.3Å. As a result, the HO distance of 1.623Å is beneficial to the formation of stable H-bond between FF and l-Trp. On the other hand, the distance between the H atom of NHCO on FF and the O atom of OH on d-Trp is too large to form a stable H-bond. It is believed, therefore, that the induced FF-based self-assembled structures exhibit a higher affinity toward l-Trp.

Chiral recognition of Trp enantiomers was also studied using a GCE modified with electrodeposited FF/CS films. As shown in Fig. 3F, the value of I
l-Trp/I
d-Trp is only 2.03, which is considerably lower than that at the FF-CS modified GCE (3.72). The decreased recognition efficiency might be attributed to the highly aggregated nanorod structure of the electrodeposited FF/CS (Fig. 3G), since the number of active sites for chiral recognition at FF/CS surface is greatly reduced as a result.

The long-term stability of the proposed FF-CS/GCE is evaluated by cyclic voltammetry. Fig. 3H shows the CVs of FF-CS/GCE in 5mM [Fe(CN)6]4−/3−. After 20cycles at 100mVs−1, the increase in the oxidation peak current is only about 10% of the initial current, and the results indicate that the prepared FF-CS self-assembled structure has high electrochemical stability. The reproducibility of the chiral sensor was also studied. A series of five different FF-CS/GCEs were prepared separately under the same conditions, and the differential pulse voltammograms (DPVs) of 0.5mM l-Trp and d-Trp included with FF-CS were recorded. The recognition efficiencies of the five chiral sensors were calculated to be 3.72, 3.54, 3.83, 3.91 and 3.67, respectively, with a relative standard deviation (RSD) of 3.8%, indicating that the electrode coatings could be produced with good reproducibility.

The electrochemical recognition of Trp enantiomers using self-assembled diphenylalanine (FF) materials induced by GQDs, CS and CTAB is investigated. Compared with lyophilized FF and spontaneously self-assembled FF, these induced FF-based self-assemblies, especially FF-CS, possess more chiral sites, resulting in enhanced recognition efficiency. Although the ratio of current signals at FF-CS/GCE (3.72) is lower than that at a multi-walled carbon nanotube-CS/GCE (5.05) [4], it is still superior to the result obtained using l-lysine functionalized reduced graphene oxide (1.24) [3], β-cyclodextrin-graphene/GCE (3.15) [5] and poly(l-glutamic acid)-β-cyclodextrin/GCE (2.30) [6]. When l- and d-Trp are included with these induced FF-based self-assembled materials, the distance between H and O is beneficial to the formation of stable H-bonds between FF and l-Trp. Therefore, these materials exhibit a higher affinity toward l-Trp and consequently successful electrochemical discrimination between Trp enantiomers is achieved.

This work was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (21775013), Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (BK20171194) and Postgraduate Research & Practice Innovation Program of Jiangsu Province (KYCX17_2090).
